did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a two-stage deep learning system for skin lesion classification using MobileNetV3-Small for skin region detection and EfficientNet-B4 for classifying lesions into seven diagnostic categories. The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset, applied data augmentation and class balancing, and used Grad-CAM for interpretability, achieving up to 94% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a two-stage deep learning system for skin lesion classification. It details the methodology (MobileNetV3-Small, EfficientNet-B4), dataset usage (HAM10000), data augmentation techniques, and reports new experimental results including accuracy metrics (up to 94%) and comparative performance tables, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: ДВОЕТАПНА СИСТЕМА ГЛИБОКОГО НАВЧАННЯ ДЛЯ КЛАСИФІКАЦІЇ УРАЖЕНЬ ШКІРИ
journal: Вісник Приазовського державного технічного університету. Серія: Технічні науки
brief_summary: This study developed a two-stage deep learning system for automated skin lesion classification to support early skin cancer detection. The first stage utilizes MobileNetV3-Small to detect skin regions, while the second stage employs EfficientNet-B4 to classify lesions into seven diagnostic categories using the HAM10000 dataset. To address class imbalance and improve generalization, the authors applied data augmentation, class balancing, and image preprocessing techniques. Model interpretability was ensured through Grad-CAM visualization, and performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics. The system achieved a classification accuracy of up to 94%, demonstrating high stability and potential for use in preliminary screening tools.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin lesions (melanocytic nevus, melanoma, benign keratosis-like lesion, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis / Bowen's disease, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion) and binary skin region detection.
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a two-stage deep learning system. Stage 1 uses MobileNetV3-Small for binary classification to detect skin regions. Stage 2 uses EfficientNet-B4 for multiclass classification of skin lesions into seven diagnostic categories using the HAM10000 dataset. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermatoscopic images containing 10,000 skin images from human subjects, classified into seven diagnostic categories. The dataset exhibits strong class imbalance, with benign lesions represented more frequently than malignant ones.

use.training: Used for training the two-stage deep learning system (MobileNetV3-Small for skin detection and EfficientNet-B4 for lesion classification) with stratified splitting to maintain class proportions.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training to monitor loss and accuracy, and for adaptive learning rate reduction.
use.testing: Used for testing to evaluate final model performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and compare architectures (EfficientNet-B3 vs EfficientNet-B4).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: Best results were observed for classes with clearly expressed morphological features, while difficulties arose in distinguishing visually similar categories.

architecture_used.main_model: Two-stage deep learning system: Stage 1 uses MobileNetV3-Small for skin region detection (binary classification). Stage 2 uses EfficientNet-B4 for multi-class lesion classification (compared against EfficientNet-B3).
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights on ImageNet were used for both MobileNetV3-Small and EfficientNet-B4.
architecture_used.input_size: Stage 1 (MobileNetV3-Small): 224x224 pixels. Stage 2 (EfficientNet-B4): 380x380 pixels. (EfficientNet-B3 used 300x300 pixels for comparison).

architecture_details.stage_1_task: Binary classification for skin region detection

training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Weighted random sampling and class weights in loss function

preprocessing_techniques: Image scaling to 224x224 pixels for MobileNetV3-Small and 380x380 pixels for EfficientNet-B4
Stratified splitting of data into training, validation, and test subsets to maintain class proportions
Data augmentation: random cropping, rotation, horizontal and vertical flipping, affine transformations, brightness adjustment, contrast adjustment, saturation adjustment, and Gaussian blur

conclusions: The proposed two-stage deep learning system, combining MobileNetV3-Small for skin region detection and EfficientNet-B4 for lesion classification, achieved a classification accuracy of up to 94% on the HAM10000 dataset.

EfficientNet-B4 demonstrated significantly higher classification accuracy (94%) compared to EfficientNet-B3 (78.5%) and DenseNet-121 (88%), attributed to its compound scaling architecture and higher input resolution (380x380 pixels).

The two-stage architecture (skin detection followed by classification) showed advantages over single-model approaches by reducing false predictions associated with analyzing background areas or irrelevant objects in real-world images.

The system demonstrated high stability and generalization capabilities in real-world conditions, supported by data augmentation and class balancing techniques (weighted random sampling and class weights) to address dataset imbalance.
Model interpretability was ensured using Grad-CAM, which visualized image regions most influencing the prediction, facilitating trust in the diagnostic process.

The system achieved high per-class F1-scores for classes with clearly expressed morphological features (e.g., 93% for Melanocytic nevus, 92% for Basal cell carcinoma), but faced difficulties in distinguishing visually similar categories.
The developed system has potential for use as a preliminary screening tool for skin lesions and early detection of oncological diseases.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (benign lesions are overrepresented compared to malignant ones), potentially limiting generalizability to other populations or datasets.
No external validation was performed; the system was only evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about real-world clinical applicability across diverse skin tones and imaging conditions.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for the reported performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).

Performance varied significantly by class, with lower F1-scores for dermatofibroma (83%) and vascular lesions (88%), indicating potential difficulties in distinguishing visually similar or rare categories.

The work was conducted as part of educational coursework using resources from Silesian University of Technology without additional funding, which may imply limited computational resources or scope compared to larger industry-funded studies.

While the two-stage architecture (skin detection + classification) is noted to reduce false positives from background noise, the paper does not provide specific metrics on the reduction of false positives compared to single-stage models beyond comparative accuracy figures.
